prompt: Your team runs a blameless postmortem after an AI feature shipped a batch of ungrounded answers in production. What is the AI-specific follow-up that makes the regression suite stronger?
choices: Delete the trace data so the embarrassing outputs are not retained
Distill the production failure into a new offline eval case so the suite can catch that regression next time
Switch off online quality monitoring to reduce alert noise
Replace the model with a larger one for every request regardless of cost
explanation: Unit tests can't catch non-deterministic regressions, so the operational loop is to turn every production failure into a new eval case. That's how the offline regression suite grows teeth and the same bug can't silently return.

prompt: Why add anomaly detection on AI spend when you already have a hard spend cap? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Anomaly detection can catch a slow upward creep or an early spike well before the hard cap is reached
It alerts on deviation from the normal trend, not just a single fixed threshold
It eliminates the need to attribute cost to any feature or team
The hard cap and the anomaly alert cover different failure speeds — early warning versus catastrophe stop
explanation: The hard cap is a last-resort ceiling; anomaly detection gives early warning by flagging deviations from the trend, catching slow creeps and early spikes the fixed cap would miss. It does not remove the need for per-feature attribution, which is how you find the culprit.

prompt: At peak traffic your AI feature starts hitting the provider's requests-per-minute limit and receiving 429 responses. Which are sound operational responses? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Retry 429s with exponential backoff and a bounded queue rather than hammering immediately
Shed or defer low-priority calls so critical requests still get through
Consider multi-provider or multi-region capacity if your availability SLO exceeds one provider's guarantee
Set retries to unlimited with no delay so every request eventually succeeds
explanation: Backoff with a bounded queue, load-shedding of low-priority work, and multi-provider/region capacity all keep the feature within provider limits while protecting critical traffic. Unlimited no-delay retries amplify the overload and run up cost and latency.

prompt: You pin the exact model version your production assistant uses, rather than always calling the provider's 'latest'. From an operations standpoint, why?
choices: Pinning makes the model run faster than 'latest'
Pinning prevents the model's behavior from changing silently underneath you, and lets you record which version produced which output for reproducibility
Pinning removes the need to ever migrate to a new version
Pinning is required to enable streaming responses
explanation: Calling 'latest' means the provider can change behavior under you with no warning. Pinning a version keeps behavior stable and reproducible (you can log which version produced an output), though you still must migrate deliberately when that version is deprecated.
